serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lamb sheekh kebab is approximately 100g (2 skewers), providing around 302 kcal, 10.7g protein, and 26.4g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Sheekh Kebab (Chef Recommendation) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lamb Sheekh Kebab (Chef Recommendation), cr_id G00010867, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat preparation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb sheekh kebab. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat dishes (e.g., meatballs, kofta) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 5.24g/100g; GL per 100g serving = (30 × 5.24) / 100 = 1.57, rounded to 1.6. The low GI is consistent with the negligible starch content and high protein-fat matrix of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080mg/100g — critically exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit lamb sheekh kebab due to its critically high sodium content of 1080mg/100g; if consumed, restrict to a very small portion (≤30g) and avoid all other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24g/100g) and low GI (~30) are favourable, but high total fat (26.4g/100g) and high sodium (1080mg/100g) require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb sheekh kebab has a low glycaemic index and minimal carbohydrate load, making it broadly suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes, but the high fat content and critically elevated sodium require strict portion control and avoidance of high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~10.5g/100g), cholesterol (85mg/100g), and critically high sodium (1080mg/100g) all elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit lamb sheekh kebab due to its high saturated fat, cholesterol, and critically elevated sodium content, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; if consumed, restrict to a small portion and pair with vegetables rather than refined carbohydrates.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb) is high in arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways; estimated saturated fat ~10.5g/100g exceeds the 5g/100g RA gate; sodium 1080mg/100g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid lamb sheekh kebab due to its high arachidonic acid content from red meat, elevated saturated fat, and critically high sodium, all of which amplify RA inflammatory pathways and tissue swelling; substitution with chicken or fish kebab is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Sheekh Kebab is a South Asian grilled meat dish made from minced lamb that is blended with aromatic spices — typically including ginger, garlic, green chillies, cumin, coriander, and garam masala — shaped around a flat metal skewer (sheekh) and roasted over a charcoal or gas flame until cooked through with a lightly charred exterior. The dish is popular across Indian, Pakistani, and Bangladeshi cuisines and is widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. The texture is dense and slightly firm on the outside with a moist, spiced interior; the flavour is savoury, smoky, and moderately spiced. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 302 kcal, 10.7g protein, 26.4g fat (estimated saturated fat ~10.5g), 5.24g carbohydrate, 2.85g sugar, 0g dietary fibre, and 1080mg sodium. The critically high sodium content (1080mg/100g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease unless consumed in very small portions. The low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index (~30) are favourable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes, though the high fat and sodium still require portion control. The high arachidonic acid content from lamb makes it a pro-inflammatory food for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. It is typically served as a starter or main course at lunch or dinner, often accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010867) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat is estimated from lamb fat composition as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (10.5g/100g) is estimated based on typical lamb mince fatty acid composition (approximately 40% of total fat as saturated fat) as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this estimate is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for ground lamb (USDA NDB 17066). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific HPB Lamb Sheekh Kebab entry are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this exact preparation as of 2026-04-16. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is plausible for a minced meat preparation with minimal vegetable content. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~30, low) based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for lamb sheekh kebab was found in approved sources. 5. The 'Chef Recommendation' qualifier in the original name refers to a restaurant-specific designation and has been retained in aliases but removed from the canonical food_name for database normalisation. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the dish being a standard Indian Muslim preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified.
